data.title: U.S. Equities: Room To Run, But Selectivity Matters More
data.description:
By John Hillenbrand, CPA

The first half of 2025 was a remarkable testament to the resilience and adaptability of the US economy and equity markets. The markets experienced a brief but notably sharp reaction to the President’s tariff plans, declining almost 20% from first quarter highs, but quickly rebounded in a classic “V-shaped” recovery. While details about tax and trade policies remain limited, investors were encouraged by indications that worst-case scenarios were being taken off the table.

Importantly, investors are now positioned to gain much-needed clarity on several key policies, including tariff implementation, the “One Big Beautiful Bill” working its way through Congress, and the future direction of Fed policy.

Even as equity markets have begun pricing in positive outcomes to these uncertainties, investors should remember that immediate success is not guaranteed, and the sharp rebound in equity valuations leaves little room for missteps.

That said, we believe favorable resolutions to tariff and tax policies will dramatically reduce uncertainty and allow companies and investors to adjust with confidence to the new landscape. This should provide sufficient positive momentum to keep equity valuations elevated in the near term.

Although the clock is ticking on the 90-day tariff pause set to expire July 9, we believe the most extreme reciprocal tariffs are unlikely to be fully implemented (with the possible exception of China). This should afford companies additional time to adjust their supply chains, ultimately reducing downside risk to corporate earnings.

Despite conflicting headlines, we expect eventual passage of the tax bill, which should provide incremental benefits to workers' take-home pay and help sustain consumer spending. Simultaneously, the bill should materially reduce regulatory burdens through the "10-for-1" deregulation protocol and provide a framework for long-term productivity growth.

Finally, we believe slowing inflation trends will make it increasingly difficult for the Federal Reserve to continue delaying additional rate cuts. Historically, rate cuts coupled with a healthy job market, solid corporate balance sheets, manageable energy costs, and continued consumer spending have provided an attractive fundamental backdrop for US equity markets.

From a market perspective, with the S&P 500 Index back near all-time highs, we are no longer in recovery mode where “a rising tide lifts all boats.” This makes individual security selection increasingly important for the remainder of the year.

Fortunately, Calamos Growth Fund’s all-cap strategy provides us with ample opportunities to access growth across market caps and sectors. We maintain significant exposure to the Magnificent 7 stocks but are also continuing to add to emerging companies in transformative themes such as cybersecurity, robotics, immunotherapy, and agentic AI.

While we acknowledge that the economy may still encounter short-term turbulence as trade and tax agreements progress — with inevitable starts and stops along the way — we believe the fundamentals of the US economy remain strong. We view any drawdowns as offering long-term investors tactical opportunities to deploy capital among high-quality companies.

Overall, we maintain our positive bias toward growth equities. We will continue focusing on being well-compensated for the risks we undertake and believe this discipline will help generate strong risk-adjusted returns.

Original Post

data.title: U.S. To Scrap Proposed Tax Rule That Could Have Targeted Canadian Investors
data.description:
Originally published on June 27, 2025

The U.S. Treasury Secretary has announced an agreement with lawmakers to scrap a controversial proposal in the so-called “One Big Beautiful Bill” that could have resulted in Canadians paying higher withholding tax on dividends from U.S. investments. Nicole Ewing, Principal, Wealth Planning Office, TD Wealth, joins MoneyTalk to break down the latest developments and what it could mean for investors.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: In a bit of a twist out of Washington, Section 899, known as the "revenge tax," has been pulled from that One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The move reverses what could have been a costly tax change for Canadian investors. So, what now? Nicole Ewing, Principal Wealth Planning Office, TD Wealth, joins us with a look at the implications.

Nicole, always great to have you with us.

Nicole Ewing: Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell: You've been following the story very carefully. We spoke to you on several occasions just in the past two weeks about this. So we have this big development right now. What does this potentially mean when we talk about tax exposure for Canadian investors?

Nicole Ewing: So this was a recommendation from the US Treasury Secretary to scrap this. It was accepted and followed through by the US Congressional Joint Committee on Taxation. So that's both the House and the Senate. And they agreed to pull this. And what it essentially does is puts us in the status quo situation that we're currently in.

So what that is for Canadians who are investing in US-- of US exposure through their investments would be-- so generally speaking, there's a 30% tax withholding that would apply. But by treaty, that's reduced to 15%. That's dividend income.

And it's reduced to 15% in your non-registered account in your TFSA, FHSA, RESP. In your retirement fund-- so your registered retirement, RRSP, LIF, LIRA, RRIF-- it's reduced to 0%. What this would have done is increased that by 20%, maybe more, depending on how the rule is going to play it out. But we're going to keep the status quo there.

With respect to real estate, there was potential that it would be increasing US real estate held by Canadians who are disposing of that property, currently 15% withholding. That, potentially, would have gone up to 35%. That's also going to be status quo at 15%.

So, all to say, current rules will continue to apply. We won't have that shock of an increase of tax that people were really concerned about.

Greg Bonnell: So even though we're at status quo, you talked about dividend treatment, capital gains impacts. Is there still reason to pay attention here?

Nicole Ewing: I would suggest there is. So whenever you are invested in foreign investments and you have that exposure, it's important to be mindful of the fact that we don't know what's going to happen, either by foreign governments, foreign circumstances that may happen, whether it's the US or otherwise. So we want to be mindful of our comfort with that.

So if this shook you up, if this made you think, "Oh, dear, this is going to have a really big impact on me"-- this would be an opportunity to reflect and think, am I appropriately diversified? Is my risk tolerance still what it was when I set up my holdings? And so, really thinking about if something like this were to change, are you set up for success as best as possible?

We can't control the future. We know that, interestingly, the Senate did not push back on this as much as some people may have expected. So we have the House Bill that went to the Senate. And had this not been removed entirely, there was not going to be significant changes to what had been proposed, at least on early days.

So I say that we want to keep our eyes open and be mindful that things can change anytime. There's other sections of the bill that could potentially impact investors in other ways as well. So, keep our eyes open.

Greg Bonnell: I often think, in even our regular lives, when we dodge a bullet, we escape the worst-case scenario, it does make us rethink certain things about how we've been going. Is this an opportunity, maybe, to talk some about tax-efficient estate planning?

Nicole Ewing: Well, so from an estate planning perspective, interestingly, what the bill also contains-- the Big Beautiful Bill-- also contains is an increase to the US tax exemption amount for estate tax. And so Canadians do, potentially, have exposure to this. They also have a filing requirement.

So if you have US situs assets, which includes US securities, and you have more than US$60,000 value there, you're going to have to file a tax return or a report with the US government-- or your estate will need to do that, to the extent you are in excess of the exemption amount-- currently, $13.99 million-- set to sunset on December 31 down to half of that amount. If you have assets in excess of that, you would be subject to US estate tax.

So what the bill does now is increases the $13.99 million-- that was going to drop by half-- to $15 million going forward. And it will be indexed into the future. So this, essentially, is going to potentially reduce tax exposure. But also, it's a good reminder for Canadians that you are subject to these foreign rules. So we don't have Section 899. We don't have the changes to the dividend income and some of the other changes. But we do still have to be mindful that there is estate planning considerations if you have US exposure.

Greg Bonnell: You always do such a great job of breaking it down in terms that we can all understand. At the same time, these issues are complex. It sounds like if someone is listening to this conversation and saying, "OK, it's not going forward, there's still things I need to think about," maybe they should be talking to someone?

Nicole Ewing: I really suggest that. These are complex rules. And a lot of people were caught off-guard with this, just even knowing that there is withholding tax when you have US exposure in your non-reg accounts and other non-retirement accounts. And so, if you weren't aware of that-- because withholding sometimes happens-- it's behind the scenes, before the distributions are made to you.

And so if this is all new to you, get familiar with the current rules. Or, alternatively, you can outsource all of this concern, all of this worry, the requirement to stay up to date, and diligently be involved. So, hiring professionals to take that worry on for you and to make sure you have the expertise and the insights behind the scenes to make sure that they are looking out for your best interests is a great idea.

Original Post

data.title: Expeditors: An Under-The-Radar Compounder
data.description:
I have a bullish view of Expeditors (NYSE:EXPD), anchored on their phenomenal returns on equity and strengthened recently by the persistent undervaluation of the stock relative to the market. While the S&P 500 trades at a PE multiple of around 24, EXPD’s is under 20; such a discount is not really warranted, as I will make the case herein.

After initially presenting the long-term long thesis in January (here), the stock has gone nowhere. As a long-time holder, I view periods like this as a blessing, as the constant repurchases while the PE is well below historical averages have proven to be highly accretive.

Summary on thesis:

I must begin with the above-mentioned ROE. Through the years prior to the pandemic, ROE averaged 20%. This period excludes the distortion from the bumper years through the pandemic, which drives the long-term average up to 23%.

Any company can boost ROE by using leverage, a tactic that is sensible to a point but adds risk. Expeditors, however, has achieved their ROE without any debt – in fact, by continuously holding a large cash position ($1.3B as of March 31, 2025), which represents “negative debt”, their book equity is inflated somewhat, causing a drag on ROE.

Helping to keep capital low is the fact that Expeditors operates a very asset-light model, effectively building a global network of shipping clients to match with asset owners. The “buy cargo space in bulk and sell piecemeal” does not require high up-front costs; it does require a wide network with as many nodes and lanes as possible.

This is done by gradually opening regional offices and slowly taking share in a very fragmented (global) industry. Roll-up strategies don't work well when so much integration and so many jurisdictions are involved, but prudent and constant growth has proven a recipe for success.

Key to earning shipping business is the company’s global customs brokerage and services expertise. This allows for consolidation of services into one provider, simplifying logistics for customers and improving transparency and feedback during cargo transit. On the ship/airplane owner side, Expeditors is a key partner that can take large swaths of space all at once.

High returns and low risk are a powerful combination and point to superior skill in capital allocation. As argued in a prior piece, I believe a key ingredient is their unusual (and hard to replicate) compensation scheme. It aligns the whole organization around a single metric, operating income, which directly impacts bonuses (a material portion of yearly comp). This in turn allows for wide delegation to regional/district office managers, which cultivate client relationships from an empowered position.

The current relatively depressed multiple is the final piece of the puzzle. Although we never can be sure, this is likely the result of a combination of pandemic era bumper years normalizing on top of the more recent headlines around trade deals and potential disruptions. I prefer to focus on the numbers and let them inform us about what is going on at a company.

Recent results:

2024 was an important year in that growth returned to both revenues and shipping volumes. The situation ends the steady decline that followed the pandemic boost and sets a new base for future growth. 2024 revenues of $10.6B were 33% higher than revenues from 2019. Earnings for 2024 were $5.75/share. This is above 2019's $3.45, but below peak earnings of $8.37 during 2021.

Q1 results reflect a marked bounce back from depressed year-ago numbers. EPS was up 26%, to $1.47, on revenue of $2.1B, up 21%. Importantly, both ocean and airfreight volumes were up nearly 10% each. Repurchases were a little bit lower than expected ($177 million) but understandable given volatility in operating environment (it was not due to lower cash flow, cash balances increased to $1.3B from $1.1B in December).

Management updates:

CEO Jeffrey Musser retired this year, handing the reins to long-time company insider Daniel R. Wall. Since it is not their practice to do earnings calls, I usually look for the 8-K on selected questions, the latest was posted in mid-May; the first under Wall's tenure.

The tone is quite positive, the volatility around tariffs is driving more customer engagement, a providing opportunity for share gains. The rest of 2025 will likely continue to be full of surprises, but that is precisely when the company tends to do best. One thing to look for during the next (Q2) release is for commentary around buy/sell rates, which have been compressing, as well as volumes, but these will continue to be impacted on a short-term basis due to tariff negotiation windows and related pull forwards.

Valuation:

As noted, PE continues to be depressed when compared to the market and when compared to past levels. This despite the low risk (no debt) and much higher than average ROE.

I believe management will continue to use share repurchases liberally to take advantage. The charts below show how impactful the past repurchases have been, increasing earnings per share from around $3 in the 2015-2020 period to $6 currently (in a weaker part of the cycle).

Share repurchases will continue to be a tailwind for EPS growth and also help keep dividend growth higher. Although the yield always looks low, the growth has been impressive - for long-term holders, it is the growth that matters more than current yields.

Keep in mind, the dividend is paid semi-annually; thus, the $0.77 level equates to $1.54.

Risks

A key question I have been asking myself as I review my own portfolio is: how will AI impact operations? For Expeditor's I believe the initial implementation is going to be decidedly positive.

Salaries are the largest expense (other than freight), fluctuation from $2B to $1.7B more recently. Although warehouse operations are not necessarily as core to Expeditors as fulfillment behemoths like Amazon (AMZN); It is good to see they have been using automation for a while (aided by AI for picking route efficiency).

It is not hard to imagine how most processes can be enhanced by AI, including routing efficiencies, pricing algorithms and package handling optimization. But at the core, the business is consolidating customers seeking solutions and purchasing cargo ahead of time and in bulk.

Other than a recession, where trade volumes inevitably slow down, the balance sheet is a fortress and management has flexibility on headcount and low fixed costs. The company is built to last and usually comes out stronger (in part by an improved negotiating position vis-à-vis asset owners who are hurt more directly).

Conclusion:

Expeditors appears undervalued, as its below-market PE persists despite their leading ROE coupled with no leverage. The situation should not distress long-term holders as the company will very likely continue to repurchase shares, already over 35% of shares have been retired - a strong tailwind to EPS and dividend growth. In fact, during Jeff Musser's tenure as CEO, Expeditors returned almost $10B in repurchases and dividends, about 2/3 of its current market cap (in just 10 years!).

Going forward, I expect EPS through the cycle to comfortably surpass $5/share and perhaps settle around $6/share. The stock, trading at $120, needs to be adjusted for $10/share in cash, resulting in a sub-20 PE for a +20% ROE.

data.title: RTX Stock Flies Past Targets: Why I'm Raising My Price Target
data.description:
RTX Corporation (RTX), a leading aerospace and defense company, has strongly outperformed the market since my 2024 Buy rating. The stock has surged 18.4%, easily beating the S&P 500’s 2.7% gain, and delivered an even stronger 19.9% return compared to the post-tariff stock price dip discussed in my prior report. These gains pushed RTX above both my conservative and more optimistic price targets. In this report, I analyze RTX’s recent performance and fundamentals to determine whether the stock still offers attractive upside and deserves a continued Buy rating.

The Bullish Case For Commercial Aerospace And Defense

The bullish case for the commercial aerospace and defense industries is quite an easy one. Demand for commercial airplanes remains strong, with Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) having thousands of airplanes in backlog. Boeing has a backlog of over 6,500 airplanes, while competitor Airbus has a backlog of over 8,630 airplanes. Boeing projects demand for new airplanes to be 43,600 airplanes over the next 20 years. This provides an extremely strong backdrop for OEMs and the supply chain.

For defense, an unpredictable geopolitical landscape with various conflict zones globally pushes defense spendings higher. Europe is working on increasing its security and defense autonomy, which does offer opportunities for US defense contractors in parallel with European contractors. At 3.5% of GDP, the defense budget could grow to $761 billion by 2030 and obviously with the new NATO targets of 5% that could rise to over $1 trillion. In the US, the national security budget is already set to exceed $1 trillion.

The Side Notes To The Bullish Case In Commercial Aerospace

The long-term trends in both end markets are very positive. However, there are side notes both positive and negative that should be kept in mind. Probably the biggest challenge that RTX is facing is with its GTF engine deliveries. Due to metal powder issues, the company has taken significant charges in 2023, but the operational impact lasts through 2025 and some airline executives are anticipating impacts beyond that. Currently, RTX is compensating airlines, which provides a financial drag and deliveries of Airbus likely driven by the GTF engine issues are also lower year-on-year. Furthermore, we note that second quarter cash flow will be affected by the strike at Pratt & Whitney. A second concern would be the tariffs. Initially, RTX expected a $850 million headwind but with tariff pauses, mitigations and USMCA coverage things are looking a bit better.

Looking at Boeing, RTX does not provide the engines for the Boeing 737 MAX, but it has significant exposure for all Boeing’s commercial airplanes through avionics and cabins. Boeing hoped to see the production rate cap of 38 per month for the MAX lifted by mid-2025, but in the aftermath of the unrelated Air India Boeing 787 crash, chances for that cap to be lifted now have slimmed.

In the aerospace supply chain, things are improving for the Dreamliner program where heat exchangers were a bottleneck, but we do note that cabins and predominantly seats have been a pain point and I believe that as OEMs look to increase production that will remain a pace item.

Regarding the acquisition of RTX’s actuation business by Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY), the Department of Justice has required Safran to divest assets. This should pave the way for the divestiture to be completed after approval in Europe and Brazil has also been obtained. The company has also reached an agreement with TransDigm (TDG) to sell Simmonds Precisions Products for $765 million.

So, the long-term bullish thesis stands, but there are a lot of moving items involved.

Defense Budget Allocation Provides A Risk

The defense end market is strong. However, there are also various risks involved. One major risk at this point is the allocation of budgets. We see big projects being awarded such as NGAD (Next Generation Air Dominance) and the Golden Dome. These programs provide opportunities for RTX through its propulsion, radar and missile defense programs with high demand for missiles as well. At the same time, we do note that somewhat against my expectations the Pentagon has reduced its requested number of F-35s for which RTX provides the engines. Congress can still intervene and appropriate more funds for the F-35 program, but in case that does not happen, there is some risk for RTX.

Investment Case For RTX Remains Compelling For Long-Term Investors

So, while the long-term trends look favorable, we also do note that besides opportunities, there also are some near-term risks for RTX that we should carefully consider when valuing the company.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The investment case for RTX is extremely compelling if we consider the company’s median EV/EBITDA. In fact, there is no upside left this year. The market is forward-looking, and so we should also consider what future years provide in terms of valuation. The upside in the years ahead is not huge, with 4% for 2026 and 14% towards FY2027 earnings. Normally, this would result in a hold rating at best. However, we should also consider that RTX trades at a discount compared to peers. I do not expect that gap to close fully, but we do note that the company has significantly improved productivity and is infusing a digital backbone that allows the organization to become more efficient. If we let the trade between the peer group and the company median for FY2025, we still have 19% upside and a $171.50 price target. The increase in the price target is driven by a 4% to 6% improvement in EBITDA, as tariff impacts are somewhat less impactful than previously expected. Furthermore, free cash flow expectations also improved in line with that, and we have the expected proceeds from the divestiture of Simmond Precision Products. We also note that the trading multiple for aerospace and defense companies has improved.

Conclusion: RTX Remains Compelling On Multiple Expansion And Long-Term Growth

While there are some near term pressures, I believe that RTX remains somewhat undervalued. The biggest item that the company has to deal with are the GTF engine issues, and that remains a watch item. However, at the same time, we do note that the long-term trends remain favorable and RTX is working on improving efficiency. All of that is happening while the company is trading at a steep discount to peers and I do not see a strong justification for that discount for the simple reason that in terms of performance and importance RTX is a major player that is not showing subpar operational performance and the engine issues do not justify a steep discount against peers. As a result, I am raising my price target from $149 to $171.50.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Commodities: Trade Optimism Boosts Oil Prices - For Now
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Ewa Manthey

Energy - OPEC+ expected to go with another large supply hike

Oil prices moved higher yesterday, with ICE Brent settling just shy of a 3% gain on the day. Trade optimism is providing a boost to the market, following the Trump administration's announcement that it reached a trade deal with Vietnam. However, this optimism may be short-lived, with OPEC+ deciding on August production levels this weekend. The expectation is that the group will go with another large supply increase of 411k b/d. Given the uncertainty, market participants will probably not want to carry too much risk into the long US weekend.

Furthermore, next week marks the end of President Trump’s 90-day reciprocal tariff pause. We could see tariff increases reinstated on some US trading partners if trade deals are not concluded. This leaves a fair amount of uncertainty going into next week.

The latest data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) shows that US crude oil inventories increased by 3.85m barrels over the last week, the first weekly increase since mid-May. The build was driven by a large decline in US crude oil exports, which fell by 1.97m b/d week on week. Meanwhile, crude oil imports increased by 975k b/d WoW. For refined products, gasoline inventories increased by 4.19m barrels, while distillate stocks fell by 1.71m barrels. Implied demand data was robust, showing that gasoline demand continues to increase as we move deeper into the summer driving season. The four-week average implied demand hit 9.2m b/d, up from less than 8.8m b/d at the start of June.

Metals - Central bank gold buying recovers in May

Central banks added a net 20 tonnes of gold to global gold reserves in May. This is 66.7% higher than the previous month but below the 12-month average of 27 tonnes, according to the World Gold Council. Kazakhstan remained the leading buyer in May. It bought 7 tonnes, taking its net purchases so far this year to more than 14 tonnes. This was followed by the National Bank of Poland and the Central Bank of Turkey with net purchases of 6 tonnes each, taking total net purchases to 67 tonnes and 15 tonnes, respectively, in the first five months of the year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Assessing The Impact Of The U.S.-Vietnam Trade Deal
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava

Trump announces new trade deal with Vietnam

President Trump announced a new trade deal with Vietnam yesterday. Under the agreement, imports from Vietnam will face a 20% tariff, while transhipped goods from third countries will be subject to a higher rate of 40%. This makes Vietnam the first Asian country to secure a trade deal with the US.

While the outcome isn't as favourable as the current 10% tariff rate, it's still better than the potential 46% reciprocal tariff Vietnam might have faced after the 90-day tariff pause ends on 9 July. Therefore, it's a bit of a mixed result.

Negative impact on GDP growth as large value-added exports get taxed at a higher rate

The differentiation between value-added exports and transshipment exports will be key in assessing the overall impact on Vietnamese exports, though it is far from clear what transshipment means. From a value-add perspective, Vietnam’s total exposure to US imports is estimated at around 12% of GDP. A 20% tariff, assuming demand elasticity of 1, could put 2.5% of Vietnam’s GDP at risk.

Many companies use Vietnam as an assembly point for manufactured components imported from China, or add enough value to the products to legally change their place of origin. However, some companies merely slap a “Made in Vietnam” label on China-made goods, making it an illegal but difficult-to-trace practice.

Studies estimate that "indirect Chinese content" in Vietnam's exports to the US ranged between 16-28% in 2021/22, indicating potential areas for further disruption.

While transshipment goods don't add to GDP directly, they generate economic activity and revenue through services such as storage, handling, and transportation, contributing to the service sector. Therefore, the impact on GDP growth is expected to be more significant than the impact of tariffs on value-added goods.

Transshipment flows from China have risen recently and will impact Vietnamese exports

Trade data for 2025 shows a notable rise in imports from China and re-exports to the US in countries like Vietnam and Thailand. For instance, Vietnam's exports to the US are valued at $132bn, while imports from China surged to $174bn in the 12 months to May 2025. Since February 2025, imports from China have jumped by 24% year-over-year. Key sectors showing strong signs of transshipments include machinery and equipment, insulated wires and cables, and computer and electrical products.

In 2024, China’s exports of machinery and electrical equipment to Vietnam totalled $77.2bn, accounting for 47.6% of China’s total exports to Vietnam, while Vietnam subsequently exported roughly $45.3bn of machinery equipment and electrical products to the US, representing 40.9% of Vietnam’s total exports to the US.

This is a remarkable shift from 2017 levels (pre-US China trade war), when China’s exports of machinery and electrical equipment to Vietnam was $26.9bn and accounted for approximately 16.6% of China’s total exports to Vietnam, while Vietnam’s machinery equipment and electrical products to the US totalled just $5.9bn and accounted for only around 5.3% of total exports to the US. Some of this change could be attributed to Vietnam’s own domestic demand, but the rapid surge on both ends suggests significant transshipment activity.

While directly gauging the extent of transshipments is difficult, the weakness in the local manufacturing sector production despite robust export growth suggests limited domestic value-add. In response, Vietnam's trade ministry has issued a directive to crack down on illegal transshipment of goods to the United States and other trading partners by electronically centralising the issuance of certificates of origin.

Recent surge in Vietnamese exports to US could be driven by transshipments

Significant transshipment activity likely in machinery, and electrical products

Benefits from supply chain diversification in the long term

While a reduction in transshipment flow might be negative for Vietnam in the short term, the persistent tariff differentials could further accentuate the supply chain shift trend that Vietnam has been benefiting from. If tariff rates revert to reciprocal rates next week, Vietnam will be at the lower end of the range in Asia – below 24% for Malaysia and Japan, the other strong electronics exporters in the region. Additionally, Vietnam will benefit significantly in the low-value-added apparel and garments sector, especially with competitors like Cambodia facing 49% tariffs.

Vietnam is seeing a substantial surge in FDI inflows from countries outside of China, particularly from Singapore and South Korea, as businesses diversify their supply chains. In the electronics sector, Vietnam has experienced one of the largest increases in export market share to the US, thanks to the China+1 production model. Major US companies such as Intel, Cargill, Boeing, and Apple have made significant investments in Vietnam.

55% of FDI into Vietnam came from Singapore, Japan and South Korea in 2024

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Policy Clarity Creates Near-Term Value; AI Drives Long-Term Growth
data.description:
By John Hillenbrand, CPA

As we enter the third quarter, we believe the investment landscape reflects a market that is successfully navigating the transition from policy uncertainty to operational clarity. The initial volatility of early 2025 has given way to a more stable foundation as markets adapted to the new administration's economic agenda, creating opportunities for disciplined investors focused on both near-term policy beneficiaries and transformative long-term themes.

Policy Normalization Creates Constructive Environment

The forthcoming resolution of key policy uncertainties has provided welcome clarity for markets and corporate planning. Despite fueling some near-term inflation pressures, trade policies appear manageable within the broader economic context. Our analysis suggests that companies are successfully implementing selective pricing, sourcing, and cost-containment strategies to mitigate tariff impacts while maintaining competitive positions.

The economy's continued mid-cycle characteristics-solid employment, moderate GDP growth, and normalizing inflation-provide a stable backdrop for risk asset allocation. Corporate earnings visibility has improved as management teams adapt their operational strategies to the new policy environment, focusing on efficiency gains and margin protection. We also expect the Federal Reserve to move forward on projected rate cuts later this year as the moderate tariff impact emerges.

We anticipate particular strength in sectors positioned to benefit from policy clarity, including financials benefiting from regulatory normalization, transportation companies adapting to trade flow changes, and infrastructure-related businesses positioned for domestic investment priorities. The Federal Reserve's measured approach to monetary policy supports our constructive outlook and allows the central bank to maintain flexibility in responding to evolving economic conditions.

Along with cyclical improvement, we expect the market to reward quality growth companies with proven execution capabilities-businesses that demonstrate operational excellence and strategic clarity. Our security selection process continues to emphasize companies well-positioned for both policy-driven tailwinds and secular growth opportunities such as AI.

The AI Revolution: A Core Long-Term Thesis

The AI transformation has moved decisively beyond the experimental phase into wider consumer and commercial adoption, validating our conviction that AI represents the next major technology innovation wave. Consumer AI applications like ChatGPT have achieved unprecedented user growth, reaching adoption rates that dwarf previous technology innovations. More significantly, for our investment thesis, enterprise AI applications are demonstrating clear revenue generation and productivity improvements.

Corporate AI coding solutions have driven significant productivity gains and cost efficiencies across enterprise IT operations over the past year. This amplification isn't merely about semiconductor demand-although that remains robust-but rather a comprehensive transformation touching every aspect of the digital economy, from productivity enhancements to entirely new business models.

We believe our diversified AI holdings strategy has proven prescient. Rather than concentrating solely on obvious beneficiaries, we've positioned across the entire AI ecosystem:

  • Consumer AI platforms driving user engagement and monetization
  • Enterprise AI solutions delivering measurable productivity gains
  • Software infrastructure enabling AI deployment and scaling
  • Data center operators providing the computational backbone
  • Hardware suppliers powering AI workloads
  • Power infrastructure supporting the energy demands of AI operations

This approach captures value creation at multiple levels while reducing concentration risk in any single subsector. We focus on companies demonstrating disciplined capital allocation with clear returns on AI investments, separating genuine AI-driven value creation from speculative positioning. Our positioning includes large- and smaller-capitalization companies and equity and convertible securities. We believe these multiple layers of diversification can provide an optimal balance between risk and reward in this core long-term theme.

Managing Risks in a Dynamic Environment

While our outlook remains constructive, we maintain a heightened awareness of potential risks that could impact market dynamics and portfolio performance. Our risk management framework addresses several key areas:

Economic Cycle Risks: Despite mid-cycle stability, we monitor for signs of economic deceleration that could pressure corporate earnings and market sentiment. Extended policy uncertainty or implementation challenges could weigh on business confidence and investment decisions.

Policy Implementation Risks: Although policy clarity has improved, negative developments in trade negotiations or unexpected regulatory changes could create renewed volatility. We've observed how rapidly policy shifts can impact sector rotation and market sentiment.

Market Technical Risks: Some market segments may face valuation pressures if investor sentiment shifts or if technological progress disappoints relative to elevated expectations. Strong relative strength indicators in some areas suggest the potential for sharp corrections.

Sentiment and Positioning Risks: Given the positive market sentiment entering the period, any unexpected developments could have amplified impacts on asset prices and sector allocation.

Our multi-asset approach directly addresses these risks through several mechanisms:

  • Convertible securities can provide downside risk mitigation while maintaining upside participation in growth themes
  • Options overlay strategies have added meaningful value year-to-date, demonstrating active risk-management benefits
  • Diversification across sectors reduces concentration risk in any single theme or policy outcome
  • Quality focus emphasizes companies with strong balance sheets and sustainable competitive advantages
  • Defensive positioning in stable portfolio components provides ballast during volatile periods

Looking Forward: Balancing Opportunity and Prudence

Our current positioning reflects confidence in both near-term policy beneficiaries and long-term secular winners, with particular emphasis on companies demonstrating operational excellence and strategic clarity in navigating the current environment. While short-term market dynamics will continue to create challenges (as always), our conviction in the long-term AI transformation remains high. We're still in the early phases of what we believe will be a multidecade shift comparable to the internet revolution but with potentially greater economic impact.

Our balanced approach seeks to provide both participation in this historic opportunity and resilience during periods of uncertainty. We remain focused on quality companies with sustainable advantages, reasonable valuations, and alignment with enduring technological and economic trends, while maintaining the flexibility to adapt as conditions evolve. We believe the fund's multi-asset structure-combining equities with convertible securities and systematic options strategies-provides the flexibility to capture opportunities across different market environments while managing downside risk.

Original Post

data.title: Sempra: Key Player In A Vital Industry That's Potentially Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Undergoing a significant investment cycle that comes with a lot of potential, Sempra (NYSE:SRE) is definitely an interesting company to look into, especially since they’re the largest energy transmission and distribution company in Texas and has significant stakes in California, as well as several LNG/natural gas pipelines and storage facilities, on top of renewables. With nice potential to grow in a vital market, a decent dividend and quite an attractive valuation, I rate SRE stock a Buy now, with good potential to see it recover if their plan works well.

We see little growth in their revenue throughout the past decade, but their operating income managed to improve significantly. Their net income shows clear improvements, although that’s not really that good for a whole decade. However, things are about to change, as the company is currently in a very significant investment cycle which they expect to be very beneficial to their EPS, as I’ll describe below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that they aren’t in the best position when looking at the current assets that are below the current liabilities, but a big portion of the latter is in current portion of long-term debt, which they just refinance (although at worse rates, and the interest is now at over $1.1 billion). Still, there’s a very significant amount of assets that cover the total liabilities, leaving us with $38.2 billion in equity, traded at a market cap of ~$49.7 billion, so a PB ratio of 1.3, which isn’t the most expensive. So, not the worst balance sheet for a utility company, and I believe it’s getting to quite an attractive level given the PB ratio.

Dividends & Buybacks

Sempra pays a roughly 3.4% dividend yield now, after 15 consecutive years of dividend increases, aiming to deliver ~50-60% of its earnings, which is not a terrible yield, but quite low in a high-rate market where the nearly risk-free US treasuries pay better. They stopped the buybacks back in 2023 when the investment cycle began. Still, there is potential to see their EPS in the long term, so this dividend could be much more meaningful by the end of the decade.

Risks & Opportunities

Sempra’s Q4 report made the stock crash ~19% when the earnings came short of the market’s expectations, cutting the 2025 guidance after citing “regulatory matters and the backdrop of a higher-cost environment,” as well as higher interest expenses after higher capital investments in 2025. Still, their Q1 report reaffirmed their updated outlook for 2025 and 2026, and helped them guide towards the higher-end or even above the projected EPS CAGR of 7-9% for 2025 through 2029, which is actually a very significant level of growth.

As part of their plan - which I also talk about in the paragraph below - they target $13 billion of investments this year, which is significantly above last year’s ~$10 billion capital investments. As per their report, they also plan to sell non-core assets in Mexico and continue their Fit for 2025 initiative to further reduce costs and improve productivity. So, it seems like they might be at the high-end of their latest investment cycle that started in 2023, when CAPEX jumped by about $3 billion (~55%) compared to 2022 and 2021. This significant potential to grow, combined with a relatively attractive valuation and exposure to key industries, are a reason why I think Sempra might be a Buy now.

Their major opportunity comes from the Texas transmission expansion plan. As the largest player in Texas’ energy transmission and distribution through Oncor, they expect to build a significant portion of the project, which is, in part, something that explains the higher investments planned. They presented a $56 billion investment plan (vs $48 billion announced in Q3; primarily focused on regulated utilities, with 90% of investments dedicated to California and Texas) back in February, of which ~$36 billion will go to Oncor, meaning a 50% increase compared to a year ago. The company already achieved record growth last year, although the 2025 guidance was lowered - as I mentioned above - to reflect regulatory impacts in California, higher costs in Texas, and delays in LNG projects. Still, their long-term goals are what I’m interested in, and I wouldn’t mind short-term pain that could unlock better prices, but I’ll detail that in the valuation segment. Of course, you can imagine how rate cuts could significantly help them with such a significant investment plan in progress, and I believe we’ll most likely see that in the near future, especially as Powell’s term expires in May 2026 and Trump (who’s already calling for rate cuts) already has some replacements ready.

Long-term, energy is literally a vital side of any society, especially if we consider the tailwinds that support it. For example, data centers consumed about 4.4% of the total US electricity consumption after tripling over the past decade, and now the US Department of Energy says it is projected to double or triple by 2028 - expecting it to consume from 6.7% to 12% of total US electricity by 2028! Of course, the other sources of demand are likely to not disappear - quite the opposite - meaning there’s a significant need for more and more energy production, which can be very beneficial for a company like Sempra. To capitalize on this, they could also potentially look into a nation-wide expansion in the long term, but I believe that would be more likely done through a merger rather than direct investments, especially since Sempra has a massive investment plan in development now and plans to divest non-core assets to focus on it. Still, the potential remains in the long-term, so that’s maybe another catalyst for long-term growth, especially if they manage to deliver synergies.

Another long-term opportunity for Sempra could come from their LNG exposure. Currently, the LNG market is in a very interesting spot following the war in Ukraine, which led to a very significant drop in EU’s imports of Russian gas and a 119% jump in LNG imports from the US in 2022 compared to 2021. This fell by ~19% in 2023 and continued to grow in 2024, with the exports of US LNG to Europe (6.3 Bcf/d) still significantly higher than in 2021 (2.4 Bcf/d). This resulted in significant jumps in profit and cash flows for LNG-related companies, with Cheniere Energy’s (LNG) free cash flow jumping 5.8 times in 2022 compared to 2021, for example. That then led to higher investments to capitalize on this sudden shift in the market, but these projects take time.

In the future, if this dependency deepens, the US could continue using LNG as a bargaining chip in future US-EU trade deals, gaining significant importance and potentially bringing more support from the state. Trump has already been in favor of pushing these LNG projects thanks to their strategic importance, with the potential to see the country’s production capacity double by 2030. Still, there is a risk of seeing further declines in these projects (which we’ve seen especially during Biden’s term), which can continue to put pressure on the market’s supply and demand balance, although I believe the long term is in favor of the commodity and I remain bullish on it. Sempra is also one of the companies that jumped on the trend, with their Port Arthur LNG project in Texas together with ConocoPhillips (COP) being already fully subscribed to ConocoPhillips, RWE (OTCPK:RWEOY), Orlen (OTCPK:PSKOF), INEOS and Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY). Japan’s JERA also joined the list recently with a 20-year LNG supply deal!

However, there’s also risks, of course, on top of the competition. We recently saw California cutting the amount requested by Sempra’s SoCalGas in their General Rate Case, approving a revenue requirement of $3.806 billion for SoCalGas, meaning ~$629 million (~14.2%) less than Sempra sought. This was 9.3% lower compared to 2023, which could be an issue if it continues in the long-term, potentially making the company miss its targets (already helped cause it to lower the 2025 guidance). For a bit of clarity, every 4 years, SoCalGas and SDG&E file a General Rate Case with the CPUC, where they propose how much money they need to collect from customers (called their revenue requirement) to cover the company’s operating costs/maintenance/improvements and, of course, a profit (the rate base). So, any cut here means a cut to Sempra’s profits, and it also opens the potential for long lawsuits with the state, as we’ve seen before. And the same for Texas. Any cut in their costs or intention to save money - which could even delay projects or lower their EPS - has the potential to lead to a miss/cuts in their guidance, which we know already that the market really doesn’t take well.

Valuation

Valuing Sempra based on a DCF model doesn’t make much sense since the company has almost never reported a positive FCF, as it requires reinvestments to secure regulated earnings. Instead, since there’s plenty of relatively predictable regulated equity earnings, I believe their earnings could be a good indicator in this case.

Using their 2024 earnings of $2.817 billion, I went for a CAGR of 8% annually for 5 years - the midpoint of their target - followed by 5% annually for the next 5 to take into account the industry’s potential to grow beyond 2030, plus a 3% terminal growth rate once they reach a more mature state, and a 6.25% WACC - in line with market expectations (see here and here). Accounting for $36.3 billion in net debt and ~652.17 million shares outstanding, the model returns Sempra’s equity value at ~$84.56 billion, meaning an intrinsic value per share of about $129.66.

That’s about 72% above the current value, which may seem like much, but keep in mind that if the market cap doesn’t improve, and they reach their growth target, we’re looking at earnings of ~$4.346 billion based on the assumptions above. The current market cap would mean they’re traded at a ratio of ~11.4, which is very cheap compared to the current 17.65 and a 10Y median of ~19.

Conclusions

Overall, I think Sempra is definitely an interesting company to consider. There’s good potential to grow thanks to the massive investment plan, they offer exposure to key markets and states, on top of a decent dividend and an overall solid balance sheet traded at quite an attractive valuation. As such, I rate Sempra a Buy now, although we have to remember that there is still risk, and everything depends on their plan to grow in a volatile market.

data.title: Rates Spark: Can Payrolls Hang In There?
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, and Benjamin Schroeder

A super-weak payrolls report can create more problems for Treasuries than face value suggests

There's quite a soft market expectation from Thursday's payrolls number. At around 100k, that's some 50k short of the normal replacement rate in the 150k area. As a consequence of this shortfall, the unemployment rate is anticipated to rise to 4.3%. And this combination comes against a backdrop of rising jobless claims, and the beginnings of a notable strike higher in continuing claims. Neither of these are at real danger levels, but they are indicative of a slow creak in the labour market. The evidence does not point to a negative print (fall in employment), but it is noteworthy that Wednesday's ADP report did, in fact, print negative.

What is quite remarkable is the juxtaposition between this slowing macro environment versus a degree of relative optimism in the risk assets space. Equity markets appear to be cheered by the prospect of Fed cuts to come, and high-yield spreads are back down at levels that point to a reduced default risk (in turn impliedly suggesting not a significant recession risk). From a US 10yr Treasury perspective, any sense of super-weakness in the payrolls report would present a recipe for a downward lurch in yields, on a theory that the economy is shuddering and the Fed may need to accelerate its rate cutting agenda. By the same token, a number of between 100-150k would be deemed tolerable enough, and one not requiring deep worry on the economy.

That all being said, we also can't ignore the Big Beautiful bill and the fiscal legacy of it, which is an elevated fiscal deficit. For now, this is being broadly ignored. But bear in mind that macro weakness would also mean a shortfall in tax receipts, adding to upward pressure on the fiscal deficit. That's an important sting in the tail for Treasuries to consider, from a supply perspective and an implied credit rating one. On top of that, we'll see upward pressure on headline inflation rates in the coming months too as the tariff effects begin to bite, which is another issue for Treasuries to be concerned with. Remember, 10yr SOFR today is 3.7%. The question is whether that is sustainable should US inflation head towards 4% through the third quarter (which we think it will). That's the complication. The gut feeling is we should be bullish on Treasuries on macro risks. But the head is screaming that this may not be the way to go this time around.

Fiscal concerns, (another) UK edition

Fiscal concerns around the UK sparked noticeable bear-steepening of the gilt curve, with 30y yields rising by as much as 20bp, some 12bp more than Bunds and Treasuries that day. It was a reaction to the latest headlines around the position of Chancellor Reeves as Prime Minister Starmer failed to provide his backing - a watered-down welfare bill had earlier this week brought into question her fiscal consolidation plans.

This also marks a stark reversal in the dynamics relative to Bunds. Gilts had previously managed to tighten versus Bunds over the past month and particularly the past week. That can be in part rationalised by Germany’s outsized borrowing plans recently outlined by the government pressuring Bunds, and gilts receiving tailwinds from Bank of England commentary suggesting that the central bank’s active gilt sales could be scaled back in the future. However, the main driver of the spread tightening, for lack of other news - until now - was a relatively higher correlation of gilts to US Treasuries, and the latter experiencing a rally on the back of softer data and dovish Fed voices.

This latest episode highlights markets’ ongoing sensitivity to the trajectory of government finances. It is something that we think also puts US Treasuries still at risk of a sell-off episode, though we are now experiencing a more macro-driven market dynamic. The German government’s plan for new borrowing of €850bn until 2029 is already pressuring Bunds, though crucially, the country is looking at a very different starting point. With the new 10y Bund benchmark that was launched on Wednesday, the 10y Bund yield is now 4bp above the swap rate. We think in the longer run, Bunds can still cheapen as the supply pressure builds. A further 10bp increase of the spread looks possible, taking into account not only the rising net issuance but also the declining bond portfolio of the ECB.

Thursday’s events and market view

The US will release its official job numbers a day earlier than usual. This is a key input to the recent discussion around the possibility of accelerated and deeper Fed cuts. The consensus expectation is still for a 110k increase in non-farm payrolls, with estimates ranging from 70k to 160k. Even after the surprisingly weak ADP figures, Bloomberg’s whisper number (basically every Bloomberg terminal owner can provide his estimate) slipped to only just below 100k. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate is seen ticking slightly higher, from 4.2% to 4.3%. Other US data to watch is the ISM services, which economists see climbing above 50 again, if only barely so.

The eurozone will see the final services and composite PMIs for June, while the European Central Bank publishes the accounts of the June policy setting meeting.

In primary markets, Spain and France will auction bonds: Spain from 3y out to 15y maturities (up to €6.75bn including linkers), and France from 10y to 30y maturities (up to €12bn).

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Groupon: Beware The Surge In Optimism
data.description:
Sometimes, amid a sharply rising stock market, the best performers may surprise you. Though it has been large-cap growth stocks that have dominated the S&P 500's performance to fresh new records, Groupon (NASDAQ:GRPN) has actually made a far more astounding comeback.

The local deals site has seen its share price nearly triple this year as the company reported stabilization in billings, and in particular managed to reverse recent trends and start adding to active paying customers in North America again. The question for investors now is: does Groupon have further room to rally, or is this a dead cat bounce for a company that has long passed its peak popularity?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Groupon in March, when the stock was trading at $15 per share. Needless to say, by cautiousness on the stock, while seemingly rational at the time, caused me to miss out on fantastic gains. And yet, I do think that the market has overreacted sharply in sending Groupon stock substantially higher in the wake of minor boosts to recent billings trends. When we add in macro uncertainties on top of Groupon's own historical choppiness, in taking a fresh look at Groupon at current share prices, I remain neutral on the company's prospects.

First and foremost, the core reason to invest in Groupon would be its cheapness as a value stock, so let's start with a discussion of Groupon's latest multiples against its latest outlook. At current share prices near $34, Groupon trades at a market cap of $1.37 billion. After we net off the $301.6 million of cash and $246.5 million of convertible debt on Groupon's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.31 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Groupon has flexed up its full-year outlook to 3-5% y/y growth in billings, versus a previous 2-4% range. It has maintained the rest of its outlook flat at $70-$75 million in adjusted EBITDA, the midpoint of which represents a 14.6% margin, and $493-$500 million in revenue (0-2% y/y growth). As a reminder, Groupon reports "billings" when customers actually buy deals and coupons on its site, while revenue is recorded upon use.

Groupon trades at 18.1x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. The stock is no longer a value play after its ~3x surge this year, despite its stabilization in North American customers and billings. Yes: the company still has an opportunity to greatly expand its adjusted EBITDA if it can truly sustain billings growth and hit meaningful economies of scale. But I think it's a mistake to invest in Groupon while it's priced like a growth stock, merely because the company had a single good quarter of customer results and a very slight boost to its billings expectations. It's difficult for me to shed my impression of Groupon as a legacy platform that is unlikely to stage a major comeback to popularity.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere while monitoring Groupon from the sidelines.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Groupon's latest Q1 results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Groupon's revenue declined -4% y/y to $106.3 million, slightly beating Wall Street's expectations to $104.3 million (-6% y/y) by a two-point margin. Results improved slightly versus -5% y/y decline in Q4.

Where investors mostly cheered, however, was Groupon's surprising comeback to billings growth - which is a strong forward-looking indicator of revenue growth (as deals purchased this quarter will eventually get recognized as revenue as they are consumed). In the North America segment, billings of $256 million grew 11% y/y and accelerated three points versus 8% y/y growth in Q4. In the international segment, excluding the Italy division which Groupon exited, billings of $80 million grew 5% y/y, also accelerating 3 points versus 2% y/y in Q4.

Another positive indicator was the company's bounce back in active customers, particularly in North America. The company added 200k net-new active customers in North America in Q1, ending the quarter with 10.5 million (+3% y/y); while the international segment still continued to lose 0.1 million net customers.

Q1 is notable for its return to overall customer growth after several quarters of decline, but we note that Groupon has a rather loose definition of "active customer" that simply means an account that made a purchase sometime in the last twelve months.

The company believes that its "hyper local" strategy of driving appealing offers within specific target markets is driving strong user activation. Per CEO Dusan Senkpyl's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Our hyper local strategy is delivering strong results with our top 10 cities in North America, growing billings by double digits. Enterprise clients continue to flourish on our platform. Looking at our vertical performance within North America Local, Things to Do, grew billings double digits for the 5th straight quarter.

We believe our Things to Do franchise is significantly outpacing current industry trends and is poised to continue strong absolute and relative performance as we head into the important summer season.

Still, it is also possible that the company had a lot of one-time strength that won't carry forward: especially if macro headwinds intensify and reduce billings strength in the back half of the year.

On the profitability front, we note that despite billings growth and a more modest revenue decline, Groupon's adjusted EBITDA declined -22% y/y to $15.3 million, or a 14.4% margin that declined -220bps y/y.

Groupon's midpoint guidance at 14.6% margins is slightly above its actual results in Q1, so the company will need to continue driving economies of scale through the balance of this year in order to hit its own expectations.

Key takeaways

In my view, Groupon's rally has taken the stock way ahead of its skis. Investors are reacting way too optimistically to a minor increase in full-year billings expectations and an upswing in North American active customer counts that macro headwinds could knock off course again. With valuation multiples at ~18x forward adjusted EBITDA despite a history of choppy execution, I'd recommend avoiding this stock for now.
